title: Two-Layer MLP Backward Pass
description:
Consider the 1-hidden-layer MLP $h = \text{ReLU}(xW_1+b_1)$, $\text{out} = hW_2+b_2$, for $x \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$, $W_1 \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times k}$, $b_1 \in \mathbb{R}^{k}$, $W_2 \in \mathbb{R}^{k \times o}$, $b_2 \in \mathbb{R}^{o}$.

Given the upstream gradient $\text{dout} = \partial L/\partial \text{out} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times o}$, derive the gradients of all four parameters via backpropagation:
$$dW_2 = h^\top \text{dout}, \qquad db_2 = \sum_i \text{dout}_i$$
$$dh = \text{dout}\,W_2^\top, \qquad dz_1 = dh \odot \mathbb{1}[z_1 > 0] \;\text{ where } z_1 = xW_1+b_1$$
$$dW_1 = x^\top dz_1, \qquad db_1 = \sum_i (dz_1)_i$$

Implement `mlp_backward(x, W1, b1, W2, b2, dout)` returning a SINGLE 1-D array that concatenates the four gradients in this exact order: `np.concatenate([dW1.ravel(), db1, dW2.ravel(), db2])`.

**Shapes**: `x`: `(4, 3)`, `W1`: `(3, 5)`, `b1`: `(5,)`, `W2`: `(5, 2)`, `b2`: `(2,)`, `dout`: `(4, 2)` -> returns a 1-D array of length `15 + 5 + 10 + 2 = 32`.
reference:
import numpy as np

def mlp_backward(x, W1, b1, W2, b2, dout):
    x = np.asarray(x, dtype=np.float64)
    W1 = np.asarray(W1, dtype=np.float64); b1 = np.asarray(b1, dtype=np.float64)
    W2 = np.asarray(W2, dtype=np.float64); b2 = np.asarray(b2, dtype=np.float64)
    dout = np.asarray(dout, dtype=np.float64)
    z1 = x @ W1 + b1
    h = np.maximum(z1, 0.0)
    dW2 = h.T @ dout
    db2 = dout.sum(axis=0)
    dh = dout @ W2.T
    dz1 = dh * (z1 > 0).astype(np.float64)
    dW1 = x.T @ dz1
    db1 = dz1.sum(axis=0)
    return np.concatenate([dW1.ravel(), db1, dW2.ravel(), db2])
hints: Recompute the forward pass first (z1 = x@W1+b1, h = relu(z1)) so you have the cached activations needed for backprop.

Backprop layer-by-layer in reverse: dW2 = h.T @ dout, db2 = dout.sum(axis=0), dh = dout @ W2.T, then apply the ReLU gradient mask (z1 > 0) to dh before computing dW1 = x.T @ dz1 and db1 = dz1.sum(axis=0).

solutions.name: Manual backpropagation
solutions.explanation: Backprop applies the chain rule layer by layer in reverse. For the output linear layer $\text{out}=hW_2+b_2$: $dW_2 = h^\top \text{dout}$, $db_2 = \sum_i \text{dout}_i$, and the gradient flowing into $h$ is $dh = \text{dout}\,W_2^\top$. ReLU's local gradient is the indicator $\mathbb{1}[z_1>0]$, so $dz_1 = dh \odot \mathbb{1}[z_1>0]$. For the input linear layer $z_1=xW_1+b_1$: $dW_1 = x^\top dz_1$, $db_1 = \sum_i (dz_1)_i$.
solutions.code:
import numpy as np
def mlp_backward(x, W1, b1, W2, b2, dout):
    x = np.asarray(x, dtype=np.float64)
    W1 = np.asarray(W1, dtype=np.float64); b1 = np.asarray(b1, dtype=np.float64)
    W2 = np.asarray(W2, dtype=np.float64); b2 = np.asarray(b2, dtype=np.float64)
    dout = np.asarray(dout, dtype=np.float64)
    z1 = x @ W1 + b1
    h = np.maximum(z1, 0.0)
    dW2 = h.T @ dout
    db2 = dout.sum(axis=0)
    dh = dout @ W2.T
    dz1 = dh * (z1 > 0).astype(np.float64)
    dW1 = x.T @ dz1
    db1 = dz1.sum(axis=0)
    return np.concatenate([dW1.ravel(), db1, dW2.ravel(), db2])
